videos.title: Siberian Huskies Excitedly Pull Sleds on Snowy Day - 1277355
videos.description:
On a snowy day, these two teams of Siberian husky dogs pulled two sleds across the snow. They seemed to be excited to run and pulled the sleds enthusiastically.

videos.title: When Polar Bears Go On A Rampage Caught on Camera!
videos.description:
They roam the ice with silent power… built for survival, driven by instinct.
But when these apex predators cross paths with humans — the line between wonder and danger disappears in an instant.
From remote roads in Russia to icy coastlines in Canada… from near misses to narrow escapes…
These are real encounters — captured on camera — when polar bears go on a rampage.
Moments where a single mistake could cost a life.
This is not fiction. This is nature, uncaged.
You’re watching: When Polar Bears Go On A Rampage — Caught on Camera!

videos.transcript:
[Music]
They roam the ice with silent power,
built for survival, driven by instinct.
But when these apex predators cross
paths with humans, the line between
wonder and danger disappears in an
instant.
From remote roads in Russia to icy
coastlines in Canada, from near misses
to narrow escapes, these are real
encounters captured on camera when polar
bears go on a rampage. Moments where a
single mistake could cost a life. This
is not fiction. This is nature uncaged.
You're watching when polar bears go on a
rampage caught on camera.
A polar bear suddenly appears at a coal
mining site in Norway and charges toward
a man. With the power to sprint at 25
mph, polar bears are far faster than
humans who average just 12.
In this icy terrain, running is not just
useless, it's a deadly mistake.
But in a rare stroke of luck, the man
reaches his snowmobile just in time and
escapes.
A polar bear suddenly appears in Yamal,
Siberia.
One of the onlookers seems to throw food
toward it. A fatal mistake that could
make the bear refuse to leave.
But what happens next is even more
reckless. A man begins walking toward
the bear.
Approaching a polar bear is extremely
dangerous. These apex predators are
powerful, fast, and capable of attacking
without warning if they feel curious or
threatened.
At this distance, if the bear had chosen
to strike, the man wouldn't have had a
chance to escape.
Fortunately, this time the polar bear
was only bluffing.
A polar bear suddenly appears
approaching hikers in Ontario, Canada.
Shouting can sometimes deter a polar
bear, but it's a risky tactic best used
only when standing your ground calmly is
the safest option.
A cameraman takes shelter as a polar
bear approaches. Curious at first, then
fixated.
The bear stares for a moment before
lunging at the door, testing it with
calculated force.
>> Yeah, the door's open.
This apex predator begins probing from
different angles, searching for the
structure's weakest point. Unlike other
bear species, the polar bear is a true
predator built for the kill. Life in the
harsh Arctic has shaped them into
efficient, aggressive, and tireless
hunters.
Every part of the shelter is reinforced,
but if the bear were to strike from
above, the same way it crushes sea ice,
the outcome could be disastrous.
Weighing up to 1,300 lb with powerful
shoulders and forlims, a polar bear can
deliver blows exceeding 5,000 newtons.
Its claws are massive, sharp, and
devastating.
Moments later, the bear rises on its
hind legs, gazing down at the protective
glass.
If it chose to climb, there would be
little anyone can do.
Fortunately, this bear knows when to
give up and leaves the cameraman with an
encounter he will never forget.
Few sightes are more dangerous in the
Arctic than a mother polar bear with
cubs.
>> Get too close and she won't hesitate to
defend them.
While riding their snowmobiles across
the frozen Arctic, a massive male polar
bear spots them and takes sudden
interest.
Without hesitation, the giant predator
begins to approach step by deliberate
step.
Polar bears, especially males, are
naturally curious. Unlike females with
cubs, who act out of defense, adult
males can be actively predatory,
especially when food is scarce. In
remote regions where human presence is
rare, polar bears often show little
fear. Instead of retreating, they
investigate or confront.
In situations like this, starting a
snowmobile may scare the bear off, but
it doesn't always work. That's why when
traveling through polar bear territory,
carrying bear spray or a flare gun is
essential.
Approaching a polar bear with a
snowmobile is far from ideal, but in
emergencies and if done with extreme
caution, it can sometimes drive the
animal away. Still, it remains a
high-risk maneuver, one that should only
be attempted when no other option
exists.
In a chilling encounter in Norway, a
polar bear attacked a car.
Experts advise staying inside, keeping
windows sealed, and remaining calm.
These predators are incredibly strong,
and even a vehicle may not guarantee
safety.
The polar bear approaches its prey with
a calm, silent expression, relying on
stillness to hunt effectively in the
quiet vastness of the Arctic.
As an ambush predator, even the
slightest sound can ruin its chance to
strike. And those fooled by its gentle
looking face may face a deadly
consequence.
>> This chilling footage captured around
1987 shows a woman feeding a polar bear.
At first, the bear appears calm, even
non-aggressive. But this simple act is a
fatal mistake. By feeding the bear, she
unknowingly teaches it that humans are a
source of food. From that point on, the
bear stops hunting its natural prey like
seals and begins actively seeking out
humans.
And when a polar bear realizes that
people are neither a threat nor off
limits, it grows bolder.
Eventually, if food is no longer
offered, that same bear may not just
beg, it may attack.
Unknowingly, the film crew finds
themselves surrounded not by one, but by
13 polar bears. One by one, the bears
emerge from the icy wilderness. Most of
them mothers with cubs and toe. In such
an open exposed environment, tracking
the movements of so many apex predators
becomes nearly impossible.
Polar bears are highly territorial and
can cover vast distances in search of
food, especially as sea ice continues to
shrink.
Several bears begin moving closer.
Then, without a warning, a polar bear
charges fast and aggressive, shattering
the silence of the Arctic.
Fortunately, the crew remains calm.
Their experience in the field allows
them to deter the bear without
escalating the encounter.
This encounter took place in Churchill,
Canada, known as the polar bear capital
of the world, where each fall hundreds
of polar bears gather along the shores,
waiting for the sea ice to return.
Though this bear appeared calm, that
peaceful demeanor can turn ferocious the
moment an opportunity presents itself.
A young polar bear spots the film crew
and begins to approach. Drawn by
curiosity.
Aware of the potential danger, the team
quickly takes shelter inside their
vehicle.
But the bear's interest only grows and
it edges closer to the car. In moments
like this, stillness is survival. Even
the slightest movement can trigger an
unpredictable response.
Starting the engine becomes a calculated
decision.
The sudden roar of the motor is often
enough to startle a bear and send it
retreating without the need for
confrontation.
But timing is everything. If the engine
is started too late, once the bear is
agitated or begins to make contact, the
risk escalates dramatically.
In Russia, a mother bear and her cub
suddenly appear and charge at a man
approaching a container. Mother bears
are deeply protective. Even a slight
threat to their cubs can trigger a
sudden violent response, making any
close approach a potentially fatal
mistake.
Fortunately, the mother bear and her
cubs were eventually driven away without
further incident.
As the film crew takes a short break in
the heart of the Arctic, an unexpected
sound breaks the silence.
A massive male polar bear suddenly
appears at the window, staring in,
curious and unblinking. It's a chilling
moment. Only a thin pane of glass stands
between the crew and one of the Arctic's
most powerful carnivores. Polar bears
can easily shatter windows with a single
swipe or bite. Their strength is
unmatched among land predators.
The footage cuts off, but thankfully the
crew is prepared. In extreme
environments like this, carrying
protection is not just precaution, it's
survival.
A terrifying encounter unfolds as a man
comes face to face with a massive polar
bear on a remote road.
In moments like this, running is the
worst mistake. Instead, slowly back away
while keeping your eyes on the bear. If
it approaches, raise your arms, speak
firmly, and make loud noises to show
you're not easy prey.
Those were some of the most intense
polar bear rampages ever captured on
camera. Which moment left you holding
your breath? Tell us in the comments
below if you found this video thrilling.
Make sure to hit that like button,
subscribe, and ring the bell so you can
never miss another heartpounding
encounter. Craving more wild moments
from the edge of survival? Check out one
of these next videos. We've lined up two
jaw-dropping stories you won't believe.

videos.title: Easter time ski at Stokley Creek - Part 2
videos.description:
Video by Vince Call. The Peterson trail out to Bone Lake and the warming hut at Norms cabin was exceptionally firm, and fast. With a short break at the warming hut at noon, we headed out on the Pickard Lake trail shaded from the north slope remained firm and fast. We took the new trail heading north on the logging road for a quick 2K run which led over to Sayer Lake and the upper lakes of Bella and Evans. The short trail on the east side of Sayer was starting to melt off in the days of ongoing sunshine.

The view above Bella to the west and the Haviland Bay area of Superior was clear, blue skies and the sounds of running water as the sunshine worked the upper iced ridges. The ski around Bella over to Evans lake found a few blow downs that required stepping out of the skies and into the knee deep snow.

We headed back on the main Walker lake trail and took the cut over in the valley below the King Mountain cabin inorder to avoid home run hill, which was burning off in the afternoon sun. skiing back along the raging waters of Stokely Creek brought us back out behind the lodge and return to the parking lot at 4pm. Amazingly enough the snow remained firm but for the inch or so of corn snow made by the mid afternoon temps in the upper 30F, to lower 40F.

videos.title: I Went To The Actual South Pole (Nobody Goes Here)
videos.description:
Who is ready for a WILD adventure to the world's most remote place?! Even though I may have traveled to all 197 countries, THIS IS MY BIGGEST TRIP EVER in terms of planning, production costs and overall epicness! Join me on the adventure of a lifetime to visit the ACTUAL, GEOGRAPHIC SOUTH POLE -- a place where less than 3,000 humans in history have ever stepped foot.  

If you want to win 1 of 5 signed postcards from Antarctica, comment below and tell me A) your favorite part of the video, B) where you are watching from and C) your bucket list trip!  Bonus points if you are creative!  I ship worldwide :)

Thank you White Desert for putting on an amazing trip! If you want to visit the South Pole, then email my friend Maynard (mz@mztravel.co) and he can help organize! Serious requests only. 

#antarctica #adventure #travel 

✅  Don't forget to SUBSCRIBE to my channel by clicking here ➞   https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC0Ize0RLIbGdH5x4wI45G-A

▶️ Watch my favorite videos ➞ https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLhiP3P44ul3Y8LDbhXJmhVsVWcpe-7O4O

Thank you to Andrew Macdonald for the amazing drone & nature shots!! Check out his work: https://www.instagram.com/a_mac_photo/

FIND ME ON:
► INSTAGRAM: https://www.instagram.com/drewbinsky/
► TIK TOK: https://www.tiktok.com/@drewbinsky
► FACEBOOK: https://www.facebook.com/drewbinsky/ 
► YOUTUBE 2ND CHANNEL: https://rb.gy/a65sj

👨🏻‍🦰 WHO AM I? 
I'm Drew Binsky and I have been to EVERY country in the world (197/197).  I make documentaries about interesting people and cultures in faraway places.  My ultimate goal is to inspire you to travel because I think it's the best education that you can get.  And our planet is beautiful!

MOST POPULAR YOUTUBE VIDEOS:
► He Is The Ice Man: http://y2u.be/VF8dkjEdKNo
► Tallest Humans on Earth: http://y2u.be/u03kNQNclGY

► 3 Things You Can't Do in North Korea: http://y2u.be/mNsx0Nqryos
► Why is Everything Free in Pakistan?: http://y2u.be/CWeWxl7ruGE

🙏 GET IN TOUCH!
► Website: https://world.drewbinsky.com/
► Email: partnerships@drewbinsky.com

videos.title: Siberian Black Ice Race
videos.description:
Siberian Black Ice Race. Our website: http://www.extremeworldraces.com 
Follow us on twitter - https://twitter.com/#!/extremeraces
Facebook - http://www.facebook.com/ExtremeWorldRaces

Based on the highly successful North and South Pole Races, the race will offer two race distances, the 155 mile Marathon or the 379 mile Ultra-Marathon. Race categories are: Mountain biking, Unassisted (Run, walk, ski, skate) and Assisted (kiting). This is an entirely new category of extreme racing for those who wish to experience the challenge of racing on ice in Siberia, one of the world's most beautiful and harshest wildernesses.

Starting in the ancient Cossack city of Irkutsk racers will have an incredible chance to experience the unique chance for the first ever time of racing on top of the black ice in Sibera. 

Not only is the race itself an incredible challenge but once finished, Marathon competitors will have the chance to complete the amazing experience with a memorable journey on the legendary Trans-Siberian Express; the longest and most iconic rail journey in the world tops off this unforgettable experience of vast wildernesses, magical landmarks and a myriad of cultures.

We will be running a Siberian Black Ice Race annually - 2013, 2014 and 2015 Races are currently taking applications.
Dates for 2013 Race:
3rd -- 11th March 2013 (marathon) 
3rd -- 18th March 2013 (ultra-marathon) * tbc
Optional, but strongly advised, training is available at the Adventure Academy
 
APPLICATION DEADLINE: 31ST OCTOBER 2012

videos.title: Siberian Ice Marathon 2015 - the coldest race in the world.
videos.description: Siberian Ice Marathon is the only major long-distance race arranged in Russia in winter. The event is held in extreme weather conditions of Siberian winter and has unofficial status of the coldest race in the world. Here is one point of view by crazy Finns.
